# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

This is a [tree-sitter](https://tree-sitter.github.io/) grammar for the input files (`.inp`) of PHITS (Particle and Heavy Ion Transport code System). The reference manual is `manualE-phits.pdf`; consult it to understand what a construct means before adding grammar for it.

## Golden rule

**`grammar.js` is the only hand-edited source.** Everything under `src/` (`parser.c`, `grammar.json`, `node-types.json`, `tree_sitter/*`) and the `bindings/` are regenerated by `tree-sitter generate`. Never edit generated files directly — change `grammar.js` and regenerate.

## Commands

`commands.sh` wraps the common dev loop:

```sh
./commands.sh parse <name>   # generate, then parse test/<name>.inp,
                             #   writing AST -> test/<name>.out and lexer/parser
                             #   debug trace -> test/<name>.debug
./commands.sh build <name>   # generate + build the shared lib to
                             #   ~/.emacs.d/tree-sitter/libtree-sitter-phits.so
```

Direct tree-sitter usage (faster for iterating):

```sh
tree-sitter generate                 # regenerate src/ after editing grammar.js
tree-sitter parse test/phits.inp     # print AST; exits non-zero on ERROR/MISSING
tree-sitter parse -q FILE            # quiet: timing + first error only
tree-sitter parse -d normal FILE     # full lexer/parser debug trace (stderr)
tree-sitter build -o /tmp/x.so       # compile shared library to a scratch path
```

Node bindings tests: `npm test` (runs `node --test bindings/node/*_test.js`).

### Verifying a grammar change

The acceptance bar is that every `test/*.inp` parses with **zero ERROR/MISSING nodes**:

```sh
tree-sitter generate && \
for f in test/*.inp; do echo -n "$(basename $f): "; \
  tree-sitter parse "$f" 2>&1 | grep -cE 'ERROR|MISSING'; done
```

To pin down a failure, extract the offending lines into a tiny `.inp` under the scratchpad (prefix a `[section]` header so the lines land in the right section) and parse that in isolation; `tree-sitter parse -d normal` shows exactly which token the lexer chose at the failure point.

## Grammar architecture

A PHITS input file is a sequence of **sections**, each introduced by a bracketed header like `[Material]`, `[Cell]`, `[T-Track]`. Section names are case-insensitive and may contain interspersed whitespace (e.g. `[ T i t l e ]`), which is why header rules use regexes like `/m\s*a\s*t\s*e\s*r\s*i\s*a\s*l/i`.

Top level: `source_file` = optional `preamble` (parameter defs / comments before any section) + `body` (the sections). Each section rule follows the same shape: `<section>_header` + optional `<section>_body`, where the body is a `repeat1(choice(...))` of the statement kinds legal in that section.

Section dispatch is **precedence-ordered by declaration position**. `data_section` and `other_section` have broad header regexes and must stay *last* among the section choices so specific sections (title/parameter/material/surface/cell/tally) win — there is a comment marking this; do not reorder them.

Distinct sub-grammars worth knowing:

- **Material** (`material_section_body`): two mutually exclusive layouts — normal (`element ratio` pairs) vs. the reverse-order form triggered by a `den nuc` clause (`ratio element`). Nuclides are element symbols, `56Fe`-style, or bare ZZZAAA numeric IDs (`element` matches all three). `mat[i]`/`m<N>` name a material; `mt<N>` is a scattering (S(α,β)) definition.
- **Surface** (`surface_definition`): `number [transform] symbol params...`, one per line. Params are `math_expression`s.
- **Cell** (`cell_definition` + `surface_expression`): MCNP-style constructive solid geometry. Space = intersection (implicit `repeat1`), `:` = union (`or_surface_expression`), `#` = complement (`not_surface_expression`), parentheses group. `cell_properties` handle `key=value` attributes and `fill=`/lattice universe lists. Geometry continues on indented (`{6,}` spaces) `continued_cell_definition` lines.
- **Parameter / tally** bodies: `field = expressions` statements, with `extended_parameter_definition` = a definition plus `continued_statement` follow-on lines (e.g. `[T-Dchain]` `timeevo`/`outtime` rows carrying a `time_unit`).
- Directives usable in most bodies: `infl:` (insert file), `set:` (user-defined constants), `qp:` (skip), `q:` (terminate). `[end]` is the file `terminator`.

### Line-termination is load-bearing

`parameter_definition`, `cell_definition`, and `surface_definition` end with an explicit `'\n'`. This is not cosmetic — it forces the parser to commit at line boundaries and resolves otherwise-ambiguous space-separated number lists (e.g. deciding whether the next integer continues the current surface or starts a new one). Removing these newlines reintroduces cross-line misparses. Because a real `\n` is required, files whose **last line lacks a trailing newline** are handled specially: `parameter_definition` is split into `_parameter_statement` (no newline) + `'\n'`, and each parameter-bearing body appends `optional(alias($._parameter_statement, $.parameter_definition))` so the final newline-less line still parses as a `parameter_definition`. (An external scanner would be the "proper" EOF fix, but scanners live outside `grammar.js` and are off-limits here.)

### Lexer conflict awareness

Many single tokens overlap (`index` vs `integer`, `string` vs `identifier`, numeric `element` vs `number`, and any anonymous string literal like `'0'` vs a regex token). tree-sitter's lexer picks among tokens *valid in the current parse state* by longest-match then precedence, and anonymous literals outrank regexes. Prefer named token rules over inline string literals in numeric contexts, and scope precedence hacks (`token(prec(n, ...))`) narrowly — raising a widely-used token's precedence (e.g. `string`) tends to break unrelated sections. Input files may use CRLF; account for `\r` in line-oriented tokens (e.g. `c_comment`).

## Editor integration

`phits.el` is an Emacs major mode that loads the compiled grammar; `commands.sh build` deploys the shared library to the path it expects. Node names produced by the grammar are part of this contract — keep them stable (that is why the EOF trailing statement is aliased back to `parameter_definition` rather than given a new name).
